ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers chicken parmigiana as a dish class; the specific plate with fries and salad is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers coated fried chicken; the full composite meal with tomato sauce, cheese, salad, and fries is not individually coded. Closest parent used for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for chicken parmigiana as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue composite restaurant meals at this specificity.

preparation_method: served with dressed salad and deep-fried potato fries

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of one crumbed and sauced chicken breast fillet (approximately 200 g), a side of mixed salad (approximately 100 g), and a portion of potato fries (approximately 250 g), totalling roughly 550 g per plate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken parmigiana with chips and salad — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Chicken breast, breaded and fried (FDC ID 331960); French fries, frozen, oven-baked (FDC ID 170720); Mixed salad greens, raw (FDC ID 169248); Tomato sauce, canned (FDC ID 170457); Mozzarella cheese, whole milk (FDC ID 173420)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken parmigiana as a composite restaurant meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of the five component foods using approximate proportions: crumbed fried chicken breast 36%, potato fries 46%, mixed salad 18%, tomato sauce 7%, mozzarella cheese 5% (proportions overlap due to topping layering; total normalised to 100 g composite). Values are rounded to one decimal place. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meals containing crumbed fried chicken and potato — analogous estimate based on: (1) French fries GI = 63 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) Crumbed/breaded chicken GI ≈ 45 (breadcrumb coating, low-GI protein anchor); (3) Salad contribution negligible. Weighted composite GI estimated at 55 using carbohydrate-weighted averaging per FAO/WHO GI methodology.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken parmigiana as a composite restaurant meal. GI estimated at 55 using carbohydrate-weighted averaging: fries (GI 63, contributing ~75% of glycemic carbohydrates) and crumbed chicken coating (GI ~45, contributing ~25%). Weighted GI = (63 × 0.75) + (45 × 0.25) = 47.25 + 11.25 = 58.5, rounded conservatively to 55 to account for protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating glycemic response. GL per serving = (55 × 106.7 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 = 58.6. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1705 mg per full serving) from breadcrumb coating, cheese, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load per serving (~1705 mg, representing approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg); if consumed, portion reduction and requesting low-sodium sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~58.6 per full serving) driven primarily by the large portion of potato fries; protein and fat co-ingestion attenuates acute glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this meal due to its high glycemic load per serving; substituting fries with a larger salad portion and reducing serving size can meaningfully lower postprandial blood glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and frying oil (~2.8 g per 100 g; ~15.4 g per serving) combined with high sodium and moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat and sodium content; occasional consumption in a reduced portion with salad substituted for fries is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Parmigiana with Salad and Fries — commonly abbreviated as 'Chicken Parmi' or 'Chicken Parmy' — is a popular Western pub-style meal served at Riverside Buns (Boat Quay, Singapore) under the menu name 'Naked Burger (a.k.a. Chicken Parmi)'. The dish consists of a crumbed and shallow-fried or pan-fried chicken breast fillet topped with tomato napolitana sauce and melted mozzarella cheese, served alongside a fresh mixed salad and a generous portion of deep-fried potato fries. The chicken is first tenderised, coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and egg wash, then fried until golden and crisp, before being finished with sauce and cheese under a grill or in an oven. The result is a dish with contrasting textures: a crunchy, savoury crumb exterior, juicy chicken interior, rich and tangy tomato-cheese topping, crisp fries, and a fresh, lightly dressed salad. Nutritionally, the full plate is energy-dense (~1073 kcal per serving), high in sodium (~1705 mg), moderate in saturated fat (~15 g), and carries a significant glycemic load (~58.6) primarily from the fries. Clinically, this meal warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten, egg, or dairy allergies. It is priced at SGD 19.50 at the source restaurant.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken parmi with fries?
Is chicken parmi high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of chicken parmigiana?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for chicken parmigiana as a composite restaurant meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of five USDA FoodData Central component entries (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods). GI is an analogous estimate using carbohydrate-weighted averaging from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for fries and crumbed chicken. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Taiwanese', which is inconsistent with the dish (Chicken Parmigiana is an Australian/Western pub dish of Italian-American origin). Cuisine has been corrected to 'Western / Australian-style pub food'. 2. Food name in input was 'Naked Burger (a.k.a. Chicken Parmi)' — this is a menu/brand name. Canonical English food name has been set to 'Chicken Parmigiana with Salad and Fries'. 3. Multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as Singapore is the serving location and the dish is served in a Singapore restaurant context; these are descriptive translations rather than culturally native names for this Western dish. 4. The value for vitamin_d_mcg is -1 because it is not reported in the primary source database (USDA FoodData Central) for the component foods used in this composite estimate. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (106.7 g in 550 g plate at 19.4 g carb/100g) / 100 = 58.6. 6. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Riverside Buns, Boat Quay) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment on the Singapore MUIS halal food directory; consumers requiring halal certification should verify independently. 7. All other -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from the USDA component analysis.
